summary: Hormuz cierra el grifo global de energía: bancos centrales pausan, México termina su ciclo de recortes y la ciencia reescribe el ADN.
causalChain: El conflicto Irán-EE.UU. reduce la oferta petrolera global, dispara precios de energía y fuerza pausa en BCE, BoJ, RBA y BoE; Banxico cierra en 6.50% con inflación en 4.5% y el PIB en contracción; tres descubrimientos esta semana reescriben la genética, la regeneración y la energía limpia.

table.angle: Powell's term ends May 15 — zero rate cuts priced for 2026
Gold hits $4,720 on Iran peace-deal hopes, still down 10% since war began
US court strikes down Trump's 10% blanket import tax
Banxico ends 14-cut cycle at 6.50% — 3-2 split vote
Record $40.9B FDI; peso at 17.25 as USMCA review goes technical
Q1 GDP contracts 0.8% — worst since pandemic
BoJ holds at 0.75%; two yen interventions fail to stick
China exports +6.6%; yuan strengthens as central bank holds rates
Court tariff ruling lifts Chinese stocks above US peers year-to-date
ECB holds at 2% as energy inflation reignites across the continent
DAX -1.3%; Germany squeezed between energy costs and record fiscal push
Bank of England holds at 3.75%; UK retail at worst level since 1983
Australia's central bank hikes to 4.35% — third consecutive rise this year
Iron ore at $100-120/t keeps BHP and Rio Tinto in strong cash-flow territory
New Zealand growth forecast 2.5-3.1% — set to outpace Australia in 2026
Oxford pond microbe rewrites the stop-codon rulebook — PLOS Genetics
SP6/SP8 genes unlock partial limb regrowth in mice — PNAS
Adelaide team turns plastic into clean hydrogen using sunlight — Chem Catalysis

drafts.text: The narrow sea lane that carries one in five barrels of oil shipped worldwide — the Strait of Hormuz — is nearly shut. Trump declared a possible ceasefire with Iran barely alive, and Saudi Arabia's oil company warned 100 million barrels per week are missing from global supply. Oil is above $103 a barrel. Every country that imports energy just got a bigger bill.

The person who sets US borrowing costs for the whole economy — Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell — leaves his post May 15. Markets are now pricing zero rate cuts for all of 2026. When borrowing stays expensive, businesses invest less, mortgages cost more, and economic growth slows. Who replaces Powell — and how fast — will move global markets.

Gold touched $4,720 per ounce — a three-week high — as investors hoped a US-Iran peace deal would ease price pressure. When falling interest rates are expected, gold becomes more attractive because it competes with interest-paying assets. Counterintuitively, gold dropped 10% since the Middle East war began — high rates kept it unattractive for most of that stretch.

A US federal court struck down Trump's blanket 10% tax on all imports on May 7, ruling the president exceeded his legal authority. Those taxes were adding roughly $1,500 per year to the average American household's expenses. Countries that export to the US — Mexico, Canada, Europe, China — all benefit from the ruling. But appeals are coming and the trade policy fight is far from settled.

Mexico's central bank cut its main interest rate to 6.50% on May 7 — the fourteenth cut since May 2024, totaling 4.5 points of reductions. The 3-2 split vote signals tension: two board members wanted to hold. Lower rates help businesses borrow cheaply, boosting the economy. But with consumer prices still rising above 4% and energy getting pricier globally, the bank hinted this is the last cut for a while.

The peso holds at 17.25 per dollar and foreign factory investment hit a record $40.9 billion in nine months of 2025. Why: Mexican factory wages run about $4.90 per hour — 25% below China's — with tariff-free access to the US market. The North America trade deal's six-year review is in active technical negotiations, covering cars, pharmaceuticals, steel, and electronics.

Mexico's total economic output shrank 0.8% in early 2026 — the steepest fall since the pandemic. Full-year growth forecasts were cut to 1.2%. The slowdown reflects costly global borrowing, softer US demand, and energy prices inflated by the Middle East conflict. Two consecutive quarters of contraction — the common definition of a recession — are not certain yet, but the risk is rising.

Japan's central bank held its interest rate at 0.75% in late April as inflation climbed and the yen weakened against the dollar. The government appeared to step in twice to slow the currency's slide, but both efforts faded quickly. A weaker yen raises the cost of everything Japan imports — especially oil — squeezing households and raising the risk of a broader economic slowdown.

China's central bank kept its lending rates unchanged as the yuan gradually strengthened. Exports rose 6.6%, and both Shanghai and Hong Kong stock markets have outperformed the US market so far this year. The trade-off: a stronger yuan makes Chinese goods cost more abroad. If it rises too fast, it could slow the very export engine driving China's current stock market rally.

US courts striking down sweeping import taxes gave China's markets a lift: both mainland and Hong Kong stock indexes have beaten the US market year-to-date in 2026. China's push into artificial intelligence and improving chip-making are drawing fresh global investment. The shift challenges the assumption that the US is the only place to bet on technology-driven growth.

Europe's central bank held its main interest rate at 2% on April 30, pausing the cuts it made throughout last year. The Middle East conflict pushed energy prices higher, reigniting inflation across the continent, while total economic output grew just 0.8% year-over-year. The bank faces a classic dilemma: cut rates to support growth, or hold them to keep prices from climbing further.

Germany's main stock index fell 1.3% to 24,338. Germany's factories run on energy, and the Hormuz conflict has made that energy far costlier. The government is executing its biggest peacetime spending push in decades — on defense and infrastructure — which adds roughly half a percentage point to growth, but is not enough to offset the energy shock battering export industries.

Britain's central bank held its rate at 3.75% as consumer price inflation reached 3.3%. Retail spending fell to its weakest reading since records began in 1983. Britain's main stock index rose to 10,438, but household finances tell a different story: unemployment at 4.9% and wages growing more slowly than prices, meaning people earn less in real terms every month.

Australia's central bank raised its interest rate to 4.35% — a third consecutive increase this year — as energy price spikes from the Middle East conflict drove domestic prices higher. Economists now expect rates to hold through the rest of 2026. Each increase raises monthly mortgage payments for homeowners, already slowing household spending and cooling the housing market.

Iron ore — the main ingredient in steel — is trading between $100 and $120 per tonne, keeping Australia's biggest miners BHP and Rio Tinto in strong profit territory. The demand comes from China, where government infrastructure spending and a slowly recovering housing market are keeping steel factories busy. A deeper Chinese building slowdown would quickly drag iron ore prices and Australian export revenue down.

New Zealand is forecast to grow 2.5-3.1% in 2026 — faster than Australia's projected 2.2%. Its central bank cut interest rates earlier and harder, giving businesses and households more breathing room. Strong global prices for dairy exports and a rebound in tourism are helping. The central bank's latest health check this month flagged US tariffs and global energy costs as the main remaining risks.

A tiny creature from an Oxford pond just rewrote biology's core rules. In almost all life, three-letter DNA sequences tell cells when to stop making a protein. This microbe reassigned two of those stop signals to code for different protein ingredients instead — never documented before. Found accidentally by postdoc Jamie McGowan at the Earlham Institute. 📄 Published in PLOS Genetics · More: https://www.iflscience.com/rules-of-dna-rewritten-by-tiny-organism-discovered-in-a-pond-71126

Salamanders regrow entire limbs. Humans don't. A team from Wake Forest University and Duke University found the two genes behind it — SP6 and SP8 — by comparing salamanders, zebrafish, and mice. Disabling those genes stopped bone regrowth. A zebrafish-inspired gene therapy then partly restored limb regeneration in mice. 📄 Published in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences · More: https://news.wfu.edu/2026/04/16/for-regrowing-human-limbs-this-salamander-gene-could-hold-the-key/

Sunlight hitting a special light-absorbing material can break apart plastic bags and bottles and convert them into clean hydrogen fuel. A team led by PhD researcher Xiao Lu at the University of Adelaide showed the process works on common plastics at low temperatures. It solves two problems at once: plastic pollution and the need for cleaner energy. 📄 Published in Chem Catalysis · More: https://adelaide.edu.au/about/news/2026/turning-plastic-waste-into-clean-fuel-using-sunlight/
